sanskrit_devanagari:
ज्ञेयः स नित्यसन्न्यासी यो न द्वेष्टि न काङ्क्षति ।
निर्द्वन्द्वो हि महाबाहो सुखं बन्धात्प्रमुच्यते ॥ ३ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
jñeyaḥ sa nitya-sannyāsī yo na dveṣṭi na kāṅkṣati |
nirdvandvo hi mahā-bāho sukhaṁ bandhāt pramucyate || 3 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: One who neither hates nor desires the fruits of his activities is known to be always renounced. Such a person, free from all dualities, easily overcomes material bondage and is completely liberated, O mighty-armed Arjuna.

One who neither hates nor desires the fruits of his activities is known to be always renounced. Such a person, free from all dualities, easily overcomes material bondage and is completely liberated, O mighty-armed Arjuna.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: One who is fully in Kṛṣṇa consciousness is always a renouncer because he feels neither hatred nor desire for the results of his actions. Such a renouncer, dedicated to the transcendental loving service of the Lord, is fully qualified in knowledge because he knows his constitutional position in his r…

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.3 For, O mighty-armed one, he who is free from duality becomes easily freed from bondage. That performer of Karma-yoga, yah, who; na dvesti, does not hate anything; and na kanksati, does not crave; jneyah, should be known; as nitya-sannyasi, a man of constant [A man of constant renunciation: He is…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-3-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: the karma-yogin who neither hates nor craves is to be known as a nitya-sannyāsī — the constant renunciate. Such a person, free of dualities (heat-cold, pleasure-pain, success-failure), is easily released from bondage. Shankara emphasises that nitya-sannyāsa is an inner state available *within* karma-yoga; one need not externally renounce action to inhabit it.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.3 That Karma Yogin, who, being satisfied with the experience of the self implied in Karma Yoga, does not desire anything different therefrom and consequently does not hate anything, and who, because of this, resignedly endures the pairs of opposites — he should be understood as ever given to renun…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-3-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: the karma-yogin who, satisfied with the experience of the self implied in karma-yoga, neither desires anything different nor hates anything, who endures the pairs of opposites, is to be understood as ever given to renunciation. This anchors nitya-sannyāsa to the karma-yogin's inner equanimity, not to external renunciation.

positions.rendering: the karma-yogin who has inwardly renounced via equanimity, equivalent in inner state to the formal sannyāsī
the karma-yogin satisfied with self-experience, ever given to renunciation in the relevant sense

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three converge on the inner-state reading. The disagreement is in scope: Shankara reads nitya-sannyāsī as identifying the equivalence of inner-state karma-yogin with the formal sannyāsī; Ramanuja makes the karma-yogin's satisfaction with self-experience the criterion; Prabhupada renders idiomatically without taking a strong sectarian position.

literal_meaning: He is to be known as a constant renunciate who neither hates nor longs; free of dualities, O mighty-armed one, he is easily liberated from bondage.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna gives a precise definition of nitya-sannyāsa: it is not external giving-up of action but the inner state of the one without dveṣa (aversion) and ākāṅkṣā (longing). Such a person is nirdvandva — beyond the play of opposites — and is *easily* (sukham) liberated from bondage. The verse establishes that the karma-yogin can already be a sannyāsī in the sense that matters.
